posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
I generally run a bit of a blog on the board each year we are in Burgundy and must apologise for not doing so this year. We have been so bloody busy. Having just completed two fabulous weeks along the Cote, I’ll start putting up some notes in this thread as I get them in order.

We stayed at our usual digs in Meursault and I cannot recommend Chez Hall enough. Fellow board member, Anthony Hall has a brilliant property here. Check it out at http://www.burgundyman.com

Another fellow board member, Paul Hanna, was on his first trip to the region, despite consuming a ridiculous amount of the regions finest over the years. I think he has an unrealistic view of what sort of visits one gets in Burgundy. Our first 6 visits happened to be Rousseau, Comte de Vogüé, Comte Liger-Belair, Blain-Gagnard, Dujac and Roulot.

We commenced with Rousseau. It is not a bad thing to drink Rousseau Chambertin at 9am in the morning. Well, it might be bad but it sure tastes good. We had a concise and thoroughly enjoyable visit with Cyrielle Rousseau to kick off an epic fortnight in Burgundy. Cyrielle is a good way to start the day. The ‘17’s were being racked and only two wines were available for tasting.

2017 Domaine Armand Rousseau Père et Fils Chambertin, Grand Cru: Good generosity of sweet berry and red currant fruits. It has nice build, is very pretty and oozes minerality. Excellent potential here.

2017 Domaine Armand Rousseau Père et Fils Chambertin-Clos de Bèze, Grand Cru: Fruits vacillate between red and black. It is deep, meaty and spicy with a suggestion of moss. The finish is crisp and energetic and tastes of pomegranate.

posts.name: Jim Anderson
posts.cooked: Cyrielle worked harvest here in Oregon in 2011. Had the good fortune to dine and party with her on a few occasions. She is a wonderful and amazingly level headed person.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
François Millet has a poetic way of describing his wines, but if you listen closely enough he talks a lot of sense. He is a deep thinker and his wines are incredibly pure and transparent. We had a wonderful visit with him and tasted through all of the ‘17’s. Most of these were pre-malo but you can still get an excellent read on the wines. He explained the 2016 was the lowest yielding vintage during his tenure, even lower than 1991. He also told us it was important to green harvest in 2017, as it was a big crop. He also said that a few makers tended to saignée in ’17, he did no saignée. He used the analogy that ‘if you do something bad to your body in your youth, you’ll pay the invoice later’, There won’t be ‘invoice paying’ down the track so far as his ‘17’s are concerned. They are stunning.

2017 Domaine Comte Georges de Vogüé Chambolle-Musigny: Pomegranate and cherry fruits are so direct and pure. This is fine and exquisitely balanced.

2017 Domaine Comte Georges de Vogüé Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru: François describes Chambolle as having natural seduction and elegance. Case in point here with this wine. The malo is just finishing and it has an expressive nose of purple and red fruits and violets. It is full and sweet, with great balance and loads of mineral. One is left with the scent of red rose in the glass once the wine has been swallowed.

2017 Domaine Comte Georges de Vogüé Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Amoureuses: This hadn’t started malo but is such a beautiful wine of finesse, delicacy and structure. It has a pure and perfumed nose of rose petals, red fruits and mineral. It is direct with a high tensile spine and possesses perfect proportion and balance.

As we moved in front of the Bonnes Mares barrels, an interesting topic of conversation ensued. We generally consider Bonnes Mares as part of Chambolle, which it is, but François believes that it has more in common with say Chambertin of Clos de Tart, because of its deep fruit profile and muscle. This is particularly so with the Bonnes Mares here off the red clay.

2017 Domaine Comte Georges de Vogüé Bonnes Mares, Grand Cru: This is meaty and darker than any of the other ‘17’s. It has a heart of black cherry and good volume in the mouth. It is rich, sweet and long and flexes its sinewy muscle at the end.

2017 Domaine Comte Georges de Vogüé Musigny Cuvée Vieilles Vignes, Grand Cru: Glistens like a ruby jewel. It has fresh, juicy red and black fruits. It is perfumed, has a delicate floral spice and fabulous intensity. It builds through the palate, fanning out on the finish and possesses considerable drive and authority. A wonderful wine in the making.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: good to hear you survived your 2 week sojourn. Look forward to reading your missives.

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
'17 Rousseau Chambertin from barrel certainly was a fair way to start off my first visit to Burgundy!

Fabulous wine, bright, energetic, pretty and light yet complex - and the Beze was very good too, full of spicy goodness!

The '17’s we had sure looked good (and in both colours), but there were though enough cautious warnings from many others as to the overall quality of the vintage - but I will leave that tale for you to tell in time mate!

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Word on the rue, was that Le Soufflot was the hottest new place in Burgundy. A restaurant that marks up Village wines 20 euros from cost and Grand Cru 50 euros deserves some attention, even if the food is not great. The food is excellent and there’s a real energy emanating from the kitchen. There’s no vintage depth on the wine list but there are a couple of hundred selections that you want to drink and pricing is excellent. There is one page of the trophy things, and you can buy one bottle only for the table. A very fair way of preserving the list without stupidly listing things and having them unavailable.

To commence, a bottle of 2014 Coche-Dury Bourgogne-Aligoté seasoned the palate. It is all preserved lemon, chalk, white peach and spice. It is direct, linear and persistent. As we were heading to Roulot after lunch for a visit, all ten at the table had the short, four course luncheon menu. It kicked off with a bowl of chopped champignons de Paris, with a runny egg and intensive mushroom emulsion. It was artistically presented and whilst good with young white, would have been superb with an aged white Burg. Our second bottle of white was a cracker. The 2014 Bernard Boisson-Vadot Meursault Les Chevalières has some smoky, mineral reduction. It is intense, with citrus fruits a plenty. The palate is dense and sappy and it finishes with chalky dry extract. It was superb with the second food offering. Cured maceral with celery and horseradish. The dish has a slight oiliness from the fish and the wine provided cut and counterpoint.

An excellent main course of braised chicken thigh, under a creamy mash was served with a ball of fired chicken meat on a BBQ sauce made from beetroot. Sounds weird, tasted divine. It went beautifully with a bottle of 2014 Domaine Mugneret-Gibourg Echezeaux, Grand Cru. As with so many ’14 reds, it is ready to play right now. There’s a highly perfumed nose of red currant, plum, black cherry and dried flowers. It is full, creamy and rich. The palate has good depth and a light airy feel against the gums. It has sweet tannins, a kiss on minerally acidity and great length. Just because the Mugneret disappeared so quickly, we ordered a 2014 Domaine Robert Groffier Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Amoureuses. It had an engaging nose of flora and red fruits. In the mouth it was creamy and voluminous but has superb detail. The finish was somewhat akin to sucking on a cherry stone and this cool wine has such lovely poise and persistence.

Dessert was an amalgam of dark and milk chocolate mousse, with some chocolate biscuit and nuts. We had just enough time to punch one bottle more in before meandering down the road to see Jean-Marc Roulot and thought a PYCM would be appropriate. The 2015 Pierre-Yves Colin-Morey Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Garennes has a clean and pure nose of white peach and honeysuckle. There’s excellent shape in the mouth and a fruit-sweet heart. Buried deep in the flesh is some chalky structure and it kind of has build like red wine, in a very good way.

A half decent coffee and the making of two future bookings over the next week concluded our meal. The fact that Meursault, our home town in Burgundy, has this gem now is going to be very dangerous for us.

posts.name: J Henson
posts.cooked:
Jeremy,

Thank you for chronicling your visit as we are preparing for our first visit in July. Regarding, Le Soufflot, is that in Irancy?

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
J,

They have a restaurant in Irancy too, but this is their new digs in Meursault (the old Chez Richard site).

Cheers
Jeremy

posts.name: J Henson
posts.cooked:
Perfect. Many thanks for the prompt reply. We will be staying in Beaune for the first week of July. Are there a couple of other places you would recommend to eat? Any activities other than wine tasting (heresy, I know) that you recommend? I will be happy to reciprocate if you ever venture to our favorite stomping grounds in the Langhe where we will be in June.

Best regards,

Jeff

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Hi Jeff,

Plenty of good places to eat in and around Beaune. Try Ma Cuisine, Les Tontons, Caves Madeleine, La Superb or Bissoh. Le Bout du Monde is a good wine bar.

I know of no other activities in Burgundy other than eating and drinking. Perhaps someone else can chime in?

Cheers
Jeremy

I think my wife walked around the town while I ate and drank?

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: So which one of those was the “trophy”? At least two qualify in my book

Jeremy, if I had known you were around! We were at le soufflot last week on monday. Their pricing policy is certainly welcome as I think many places in Burgundy have gotten vastly expensive for wines in the last 2 years.

From the 2017’s you’ve tasted, how would you describe the vintage in red and white? For red most vignerons compared to 2007 but I must admit I didn’t tast 2007 at this early stage so this is difficult for me to judge. I can say that the sappy fruit will make probably wines that will drink much easier than iether 15 or 16. For white we tasted much less, the wines were ripe like 15 but with more balance and acidity.

posts.cooked:

posts.name: Monte Mast
posts.cooked:
Definitely try to get a table at La Lune. Only 20 seats and you will need to reserve early to get a seat where you can watch the chef. One of the best meals I have had period.

Les Popiettes was a nice bistro.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
kristofstevens:

From the 2017’s you’ve tasted, how would you describe the vintage in red and white? For red most vignerons compared to 2007 but I must admit I didn’t tast 2007 at this early stage so this is difficult for me to judge. I can say that the sappy fruit will make probably wines that will drink much easier than iether 15 or 16. For white we tasted much less, the wines were ripe like 15 but with more balance and acidity.

Not Jeremy, but, for the reds: 2000.

Harder to say for the whites.

posts.name: Kaushik B
posts.cooked:
I am digressing from the subject of the thread - but biking is big. If you go a bit further up north, Dijon is a really neat town. Very walkable, friendly people, and interesting history. The square around the ducal palace in the evening is magical. In a somewhat different direction - the basilica in Vezelay looks very interesting. It was in my list of places to go to …but we never made it up there …

On a different note - thank you very much Jeremy for your very evocative posts. Very much enjoying this.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
The Mugneret-Gibourg was off the trophy page Alan. A subsequent visit yielded a 2014 Dauvissat Le Clos off the trophy page at the princely sum of 70 euros.

Must have just missed you Kristof. William’s call of '00 for the reds is a good one. I think they are a lot better than '07. 2017 was a big crop and many growers did green harvest. What we tasted lacked no intensity, yet it is an easygoing, charming vintage in both colours. The whites are harder to peg. Perhaps similar to 2012 but with fresher acidity?

Thank you Kaushik.

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
The sort of descriptions we heard everywhere about the 2017’s was “airy, light, transparent, pure…”

2000 came to mind for the reds for sure, maybe with a bit more sweetness and precision, a slightly better, more focused version of 2000.

The wines certainly seemed that, lovely, pretty balanced wines - although our tasting sample was from makers right at the very top.

There was also a lot of caution from almost everyone that whilst there will be some great wines from '17, not everyone made good wines…still very early days though.

Good news was that '17 was finally a large harvest, and those in particular that green harvested and took care potentially made some good wines in good quantities.

Next time I’m there I plan to check out the aviation museum in Savigny-Lès-Beaune.

http://www.targeta.co.uk/savigny_les_beaune.htm

The same museum also has a collection of ~300 motorbikes manufactured between 1902 and 1960.

posts.name: Khiem Le
posts.cooked:

Have you tried “Le Grill de Nuits-Saint-Georges”?

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
That method of dining was discussed Alan.

Haven’t been Khiem. I will put it on the list for next year.

posts.name: Jonathan Favre
posts.cooked: Love reading your notes and trip notes Jeremy - keep 'em coming!!!

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Thanks Jonathan.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Louis-Michel Liger-Belair is a thoroughly engaging fellow, who just happens to make some of the greatest wines on the planet. It is always fun to taste with him and this year particularly so. There was a contingent from America and our group of Aussies, making for a kind of mini United Nations of wine. One of the ‘Septic Tanks’ was quite loud and funny, although I don’t think he was trying to be funny. He was asking Louis-Michel about a ‘bladder press’. Louis-Michel asked what that was. I was trying to be funny and said ‘its what happens when you need to take a petite pee pee’. The Aussies laughed, Americans didn’t laugh, and I don’t think Louis-Michel heard me.

We kicked off the tasting with a very fine white.

2014 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Clos des Grandes Vignes: Has delicious white peach fruit. There’s a lemon sherbet quality to the wine and it has excellent shape and length.

We then moved through a selection of outstanding 2016 reds. Louis-Michel recounted a quote that Henri Jayer once told him, ‘there is no sun in the cellar’. Meaning, get your fruit ripe on the vine. His ‘16’s are perfectly ripe.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Aux Cras: This vineyard was planted between 1921 and 1923. There are complex aromatics of sweet berry, menthol, sarsaparilla root and earth. It is full, deep and velvety, building through the palate and finishing quite savoury. Lovely balance.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Clos des Grandes Vignes: This vineyard was devastated by frost in ’16 and the yields were so small that there will not be a commercial release. It is super intense with dark cherry, floral spice and earthy aromas and flavours. Tannins are sweet and the long finishes is flecked with minerals.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Aux Reignots: It draws you in with its intoxicating perfume. I commented that there was plenty of Asian spice to the wine and Louis-Michel said he put it in there because he has a good market in Asia. I didn’t notice any Polish spice. There’s a suggestion of menthol and the dark cherry fruit has great intensity. The palate is silky and detailed, and it has concentration and phenomenal length without any heaviness.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Clos Vougeot, Grand Cru: Very much red fruited with some aniseed spice. It is tangy and rich with good underlying structure and plenty of muscle to the back-end.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Echezeaux, Grand Cru: Intoxicating nose of Chinese 5 spice powder and perfectly ripe red and black fruits. There was a grating of fresh ginger too. There’s great volume and intensity in the mouth with serious mid-palate sap and a super long finish tasting of salty plum.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée, Grand Cru: Great complexity, finesse, depth and clarity. You get notes of pomegranate, aniseed, cherry and mineral. The palate has a creaminess to it but you feel every rocky bump of the wine. It is silky, voluminous and ethereal, with great mineral drive and a finish that really fans out. Brilliant!

Louis-Michel was keen to check in and see how his 2012’s are coming along. So were we.

2012 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Vosne-Romanée La Colombiere: As one of our American friends put it ‘this is ready to drive straight off the lot’. It is expressive with plenty of spice and earth and a very pretty scent of rose petals. Red berry fruits envelop the mouth and it is plump with mineral cut.

2012 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Aux Reignots: Quite compact, tight and intense. There’s a whiff of ginger, compost, red currant and rose petal. It is silky in the mouth with great line. It builds and is expansive on the finish. Fabulous wine but needs a few more years in the cellar.

2012 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Echezeaux, Grand Cru: More open than the Reignots. Expressive nose of Asian spice, cherry, pomegranate and raspberry. It is rich and creamy but with plenty of mineral detail. The finish is super long.

2012 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée, Grand Cru: This close to perfection. The nose draws you in with the perfume of rose petals, aniseed, black cherries and fresh lilies. It has a cool centre and is complex, layered and expansive. There’s latent power, exquisite balance and proportion a lacy, sensual texture and length to burn. Simply wonderful.

Louis-Michel was on a roll and decided we should finish the tasting with an ’08.

2008 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée, Grand Cru: There’s a hint of forest floor development sneaking in. It has plenty of berry, cherry and earth. The palate is super spicy, with a cool mineral centre. Fruits are deliciously crunchy and the finish is stony and long.

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked: Sounds like a nice roll to be part of!

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: urologists would have laughed. Urine good company with us and pee jokes.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: You should have been there Alan. I’m sure you would have laughed.

posts.name: Sanjay Nandurkar
posts.cooked: Nice to read you are just the middle man converting the La Romanee in to a pee.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Wine is only ever on loan Sanjay.

posts.name: Sanjay Nandurkar
posts.cooked: Alan could claim tax deduction for his bladder washouts

Yeah, you were with the wrong Americans.

I have yet to try a Ligier-Belair La Romanée, though luckily have been on hand for some stunning mature examples from other stewards.

posts.name: alan weinberg

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

We then moved through a selection of outstanding 2016 reds. Louis-Michel recounted a quote that Henri Jayer once told him, ‘there is no sun in the cellar’. Meaning, get your fruit ripe on the vine. His ‘16’s are perfectly ripe.

Given the phrase ‘sunshine in a bag’ is often used to describe the sugar used in chaptalization, I wonder if there’s some ludic humor at work here from Henri / Louis-Michel…

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

There was a contingent from America and our group of Aussies, making for a kind of mini United Nations of wine. I was trying to be funny and said ‘its what happens when you need to take a petite pee pee’. The Aussies laughed, Americans didn’t laugh, and I don’t think Louis-Michel heard me.

We kicked off the tasting with a very fine white.

2014 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Clos des Grandes Vignes: Has delicious white peach fruit. There’s a lemon sherbet quality to the wine and it has excellent shape and length.

We then moved through a selection of outstanding 2016 reds. Louis-Michel recounted a quote that Henri Jayer once told him, ‘there is no sun in the cellar’. Meaning, get your fruit ripe on the vine. His ‘16’s are perfectly ripe.

It is super intense with dark cherry, floral spice and earthy aromas and flavours. Tannins are sweet and the long finishes is flecked with minerals.

There’s a suggestion of menthol and the dark cherry fruit has great intensity. The palate is silky and detailed, and it has concentration and phenomenal length without any heaviness.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Clos Vougeot, Grand Cru: Very much red fruited with some aniseed spice. It is tangy and rich with good underlying structure and plenty of muscle to the back-end.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Echezeaux, Grand Cru: Intoxicating nose of Chinese 5 spice powder and perfectly ripe red and black fruits. There was a grating of fresh ginger too. There’s great volume and intensity in the mouth with serious mid-palate sap and a super long finish tasting of salty plum.

2016 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée, Grand Cru: Great complexity, finesse, depth and clarity. You get notes of pomegranate, aniseed, cherry and mineral. The palate has a creaminess to it but you feel every rocky bump of the wine. It is silky, voluminous and ethereal, with great mineral drive and a finish that really fans out. Brilliant!

Louis-Michel was keen to check in and see how his 2012’s are coming along. So were we.

2012 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Vosne-Romanée La Colombiere: As one of our American friends put it ‘this is ready to drive straight off the lot’. It is expressive with plenty of spice and earth and a very pretty scent of rose petals. Red berry fruits envelop the mouth and it is plump with mineral cut.

2012 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Aux Reignots: Quite compact, tight and intense. There’s a whiff of ginger, compost, red currant and rose petal. It is silky in the mouth with great line. It builds and is expansive on the finish. Fabulous wine but needs a few more years in the cellar.

2012 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair Echezeaux, Grand Cru: More open than the Reignots. Expressive nose of Asian spice, cherry, pomegranate and raspberry. It is rich and creamy but with plenty of mineral detail. The finish is super long.

2012 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée, Grand Cru: This close to perfection. The nose draws you in with the perfume of rose petals, aniseed, black cherries and fresh lilies. It has a cool centre and is complex, layered and expansive. There’s latent power, exquisite balance and proportion a lacy, sensual texture and length to burn. Simply wonderful.

Louis-Michel was on a roll and decided we should finish the tasting with an ’08.

2008 Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair La Romanée, Grand Cru: There’s a hint of forest floor development sneaking in. It has plenty of berry, cherry and earth. The palate is super spicy, with a cool mineral centre. Fruits are deliciously crunchy and the finish is stony and long.

By far my favorite tasting of the whole trip!

Louis-Michel is a great bloke, very funny, but his wines are just out of this world, IMHO right up with the absolute very best wines for their level(s) made today (and yes, La Romanee is up there with La Tache and Romanee Conti, and maybe even better!)

The '16’s were superb, shame the amounts are so low. Across the range the Reignots stood out, this is one wine that really is serious GC quality, and the '16 was a cracker (as was the '12)! It was also interesting to see how approachable almost all of these were young, so silky and with such lovely poise and balance, yet more than enough stuffing to really age. Delicious wines!

The Echezeaux were likewise very, very good (easily amongst the very best Ech. made), whilst the '12 La Romanee was up there in my 5 all time great wines, this is a bottle that in 10-20 years will be simply magical - a truly stunning wine and easily my WOTY for 2018 so far!

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: Liger to me is probably the best red burgundy producer when considering the complete range of village to grand cru. No real misses. Probably cause the house style is so strong (and delicious).

alan weinberg:

bought a b of Bouchard 01 La Romanée recently and then read it sucked. Anybody w experience?

I don’t know that wine, but the 97 sang beautifully 8-9 months ago…

posts.name: Brian Heslop
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Holmes:

Le Soufflot

Enjoying your posts Jeremy.

Le Soufflot looks great, do you need to book or are “walk-ins” a possibility?

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
billnanson:
alan weinberg:

bought a b of Bouchard 01 La Romanée recently and then read it sucked. Anybody w experience?

I don’t know that wine, but the 97 sang beautifully 8-9 months ago…

LA ROMANÉE 2001 was the last vintage vinified by Regis FOREY and elevated (and bottled) by Bouchard P&F.
Louis-Michel Liger-Belair had nothing to do with it. It is however a good to very good LR, but in the usual (slightly) rustic style of Forey (+Bouchard).

LM Liger-Belair took over the La Romanée-vineyard with the 2002 vintage.
He worked the vineyard and vinified the wine from 2002 onwards.
However from 2002 to 2005 he still had to transfer 50% to Bouchard P&F - usually in their own new barrels with a slightly different toasting. So there are two different bottlings 2002-2005, but according to Louis-Michel the differences are not huge.
Since 2006 there is only ONE LR-bottling by Comte Liger-Belair.

BTW: the 1997 LR (Bouchard) is a fine effort for the vintage, fully mature and delicious, if slightly lacking the class of a better vintage (like 1999).
However all vintages from 2002 onwards are a step up …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

bought a b of Bouchard 01 La Romanée recently and then read it sucked. Anybody w experience?

Alan,

I had both the '01 and '02 La Romanees a few years ago, and fair to say they were ok, but not what you would call great. Rustic is probably a good word, and they do lack the refinement and class of the wine that Liger-Belair is making now, which seem to have been getting better and better since 2006/7, with each vintage going up another level.

Pricing on these if you look also seems to reflect this.

Might be able to shed some more light soon, as next week we are doing a La Romanee dinner!

bought a b of Bouchard 01 La Romanée recently and then read it sucked. Anybody w experience?

Alan,

I had both the '01 and '02 La Romanees a few years ago, and fair to say they were ok, but not what you would call great. Rustic is probably a good word, and they do lack the refinement and class of the wine that Liger-Belair is making now, which seem to have been getting better and better since 2006/7, with each vintage going up another level.

Pricing on these if you look also seems to reflect this.

Might be able to shed some more light soon, as next week we are doing a La Romanee dinner!

Rustic is the best descriptor for those Bouchard La Romanee. It has a jagged edge on the palate from the tannin and earth.

posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.cooked:
Gerhard P.:
billnanson:
alan weinberg:

bought a b of Bouchard 01 La Romanée recently and then read it sucked. Anybody w experience?

I don’t know that wine, but the 97 sang beautifully 8-9 months ago…

LA ROMANÉE 2001 was the last vintage vinified by Regis FOREY and elevated (and bottled) by Bouchard P&F.
Louis-Michel Liger-Belair had nothing to do with it. It is however a good to very good LR, but in the usual (slightly) rustic style of Forey (+Bouchard).

LM Liger-Belair took over the La Romanée-vineyard with the 2002 vintage.
He worked the vineyard and vinified the wine from 2002 onwards.
However from 2002 to 2005 he still had to transfer 50% to Bouchard P&F - usually in their own new barrels with a slightly different toasting. So there are two different bottlings 2002-2005, but according to Louis-Michel the differences are not huge.
Since 2006 there is only ONE LR-bottling by Comte Liger-Belair.

BTW: the 1997 LR (Bouchard) is a fine effort for the vintage, fully mature and delicious, if slightly lacking the class of a better vintage (like 1999).
However all vintages from 2002 onwards are a step up …

You took my English too literally Gerhard - I’ve been visiting and buying from Louis-Michel every year since 2003 - I know the history.
I would very much hope that a 99 is better than a 97…

I don’t know the 2001 in the biblical sense - maybe that will give you more fun for research… anyway, I meant that I haven’t drunk one, yet…

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Have been trying for years to get a visit to Coche to no avail. I guess I’ll have to tag along with Herwig at some stage. Anyway, we do our own Coche tasting throughout the restaurants of France whenever we find them at a reasonable price and had a terrific run during our recent 4-week trip. The '12 Coche whites are seriously good.

2015 Coche-Dury Bourgogne Blanc: It had some petrol and smoky mineral reduction at first but opened up beautifully. There were rich peach fruits and plenty of spice. It was unctuous and full but by no means heavy. It had 1er Cru intensity and a finish that was precise and long.

2012 Coche-Dury Meursault 1er Cru Les Perrières: Has a ripe and expressive nose of peach, almond butter, honeysuckle and spice. As it hits the mouth orchard fruit flavour floods every crevice. It is generous, charming and unctuous and its minerality kicks in on the back-end providing freshness and cut. It has awesome power and length is superb.

2012 Coche-Dury Meursault Les Rougeots: Rich and accessible. Sappy orchard fruits were dense and coated the mouth with flavour. It had a touch of almond butter and good mineral intensity. Length of flavour was seriously good, and it had great drive and presence.

2012 Coche-Dury Meursault:The standard 2012 Coche-Dury Meursault village wine didn’t have the richness of the Rougeots but did have more off the usual struck match. It was very fresh, with excellent detail and a line of citrus and mineral. It finished with outstanding cut and left a mineral calling card.

2011 Coche-Dury Meursault 1er Cru Caillerets: Extremely young and fresh. There’s some smoky reduction to the aroma along with intense white peach and fennel. The palate is direct and sappy, with loads of chalk and some plant matter. Acidity is piecing and length imposing.

2011 Coche-Dury Bourgogne Blanc: It had the usual Coche mineral/petrol reduction. There was dense sappy peach fruit and a touch of spice. It was fresh with a tangy citrus finish.

2009 Coche-Dury Bourgogne Rouge: Drinks like a Village wine, priced like a 1er Cru. Very pleasant nose of red berries, cherries and earth. It has good flesh in the mouth overlaying a stony base. There’s reasonable depth and whilst tannins are relatively soft the acidity is bright and breezy.

posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked:
That would be the day , finding myself in a cellar with Aussies !
Coche is very difficult to visit , especially lately and with what is going on there now , it will probably be even more difficult . The good days are over , that is for sure .

posts.name: alan weinberg

Nick Gangas:

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: yes. Sons take over all the time. Herwig’s note seems ominous.

posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked:
The son has an affair… does not go well with the father … trouble in paradise
I am not going to gossip further but it is pretty hefty stuff

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: LOL - that’s already a tonne of gossip . OTOH, it’s not really “hefty stuff” in the context of this board unless it impacts the quality or quantity of the wines!

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Claudine Blain is always kind and generous with her time and wines and our visit this year was no exception. She seemed genuinely pleased with the quantities and quality of the ‘17’s in her cellar and we thoroughly enjoyed tasting through them.

2017 Blain-Gagnard Chassagne-Montrachet: This has some declassified 1er Cru material included. The nose is already expressive from cask, with notes of white flowers, white peach and spice. It has good richness for its level and a fresh finish.

2017 Blain-Gagnard Puligny-Montrachet: Generous orchard fruit aromas and flavours are trimmed with spice. It is bright with nice underlying minerality.

2017 Blain-Gagnard Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Clos St. Jean: Nice mineral line. Has a fine, floral delicacy to it. The finish has sappy white peach and saline minerality.

2017 Blain-Gagnard Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru La Boudriotte: Loaded with fresh peach fruits and possesses an unctuous feel in the mouth. It has good presence and a finish laden with chalk.

2017 Blain-Gagnard Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Morgeot: Quite rich, powerful and long. It has notes of preserved lemon, citrus blossom and white peach. There is a saline quality to the finish.

2017 Blain-Gagnard Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Caillerets: Tight and closed for now. There’s a bare whiff of mineral and white peach. It is direct and stony in the mouth. There’s good finesse and build and it has excellent length.

2017 Blain-Gagnard Criots-Bâtard-Montrachet, Grand Cru: The nose is engaging, with ripe peach and spice. It is expressive and expansive, with a sappy texture and good build. It is powerful and very long.

2017 Blain-Gagnard Bâtard-Montrachet, Grand Cru: This is seriously good Bâtard of power and elegance. Pure white peach fruit is explosive, sappy and intense. It is layered and chewy but has that light airy feel that the great Grand Crus possess. It has excellent balance and proportion.
We finished with an ’11 from the cellar.

2011 Blain-Gagnard Criots-Bâtard-Montrachet, Grand Cru: Complex aromatics of peach, custard apple, honey and mineral. It is rich and creamy in the mouth, with good volume and a fine and fresh finish that drives on.

posts.name: Kelly Walker
posts.cooked: We had an amazing visit back in June with Jean-Francois. Barrel tasted all the 2016s and then blind tasted a bunch of older vintages including the 2015 CC. A bucket list visit for sure.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: any premox talk? I have avoided this domaine but would revisit if I knew more.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Alan, we didn’t talk premox with Claudine this year, but I have to say they have always been forthright admitting that they have had a problem in the past.

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

any premox talk? I have avoided this domaine but would revisit if I knew more.

Lovely wines to drink on the young side, and the '17’s looked pretty and very approachable - I wouldn’t hold them long though.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Paul / Jeremy - have you had any BG 14s recently? I took a gamble on a few because of the vintage, but not sure how long to hold for the GCs.

Of course, this luck is balanced by Jadot and an 07 Corton Charlemage from a lesser known source that has essentially gone down the drain one by one the past few weeks…

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
Always one of the highlights of my reading year on the board, Jeremy. Chapeau!

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
I generally find something snarky to say about the other 6 foot 4 Jeremy. He did suggest on Instagram that ‘when in Rome, eat Chinese food ‘when he saw a recent post of us drinking nothing but Burgundy in Alsace, but nothing he said or did during our visit could be criticised. He was in very good form and given the quality of the ‘16’s we tasted he can be justifiably proud of what they made. There was no Gruenchers produced in ’16.

2016 Domaine Dujac Morey St. Denis: Cherry fruit overlays a mineral base. It has excellent stony detail and some grip to the finish.

2016 Domaine Dujac Morey St. Denis 1er Cru: A very pretty nose of violets, roses and red fruits. It has good mid-palate richness and excellent length.

2016 Domaine Dujac Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Les Beaux Monts: Very much red fruited. It is silky and chiselled. Aromas and flavours are expansive, then draw into a mineral point before fanning out with floral nuance. It is very persistent.

2016 Domaine Dujac Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Aux Malconsorts: Red and black fruits are tinged with floral spice. It is velvety and voluminous, with good underlying mineral tension. Length is fabulous.

2016 Domaine Dujac Charmes-Chambertin, Grand Cru: There’s good depth of red berry and plum fruits here. It has some earthiness and a strand of licorice weaving through the flavour profile. It is creamy and finishes tight with plenty of savoury action.

2016 Domaine Dujac Clos St. Denis, Grand Cru: Gorgeous nose of blue fruits, dried flowers, mineral and musk. Perfumed fruits envelop the mouth and it has so much mineral detail. Balance is exquisite. A truly great CSD.

2016 Domaine Dujac Clos de la Roche, Grand Cru: Rich, black fruits are back-lit by bright mineral acidity. It is intense and deep, with great depth and balance. It needs serious time in the cellar and has great length of flavour.

1996 Domaine Dujac Clos de la Roche, Grand Cru: Very floral, smoky, stemsy nose. It is sweet and vinous in the mouth with a touch of beef stock development. There’s good complexity and plenty of mineral crunch.

1995 Domaine Dujac Clos St. Denis, Grand Cru: This is really drinking well. Has some earthy, root vegetable notes along with beef stock, sandalwood and spiced plum. There’s a line of fresh acidity and some grip to the long finish.

2015 Domaine Dujac Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Combettes: Ripe aromas of candied pear and peach mix it up with some aniseed spice. It is rich and mouthcoating, with good depth. It is structured wine with good length.

posts.name: J Wei
posts.cooked: Great report Jeremy. . . curious, why was there no Gruenchers produced in 2016? Is that the only wine they did not make from their usual portfolio?

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: such a class act at Dujac and one stop Cote de Nuits shopping! Such holdings

posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked: I really enjoyed those 2016s at Dujac and that 2015 Puligny. The entire 2016 lineup was impressive from top to bottom (didn’t get to taste Malconsorts as it was not racked it). That tremendous balance you keep mentioning sums up my impression of 2016 Dujac. Thank you for the wonderful notes.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:

posts.name: Charles Zedlewski
posts.cooked:

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
As is always the case, the welcome and generosity shown at Domaine Roulot was second to none. Many of the vineyards were hard hit by frost in ’16 and there will be no village Meursault or Meix Chaveaux. Tillets is being re-planted so that exasperates the lack of wine problem. Some bought in fruit was used for the Bourgogne, to plump out volume, but the wine is as good as ever. The rest of the ‘16’s are splendid.

2016 Jean-Marc Roulot Bourgogne Blanc: Tangy and fresh with white peach and lemon fruits a plenty. Nice line and cut.

2016 Domaine Roulot Meursault Les Vireuils: The nose is essence of citrus. The palate is the same and there’s intense minerality lurking just below the surface. Excellent precision and persistence.

2016 Domaine Roulot Meursault Les Luchets: Very fine aromas of white flowers, blossom, peach and toast. It is rich but airy, not heavy in the slightest. The finish is so long and this is one of the greatest young Luchets I have tasted.

2016 Domaine Roulot Meursault ‘A Mon Plaisir - Clos du Haut Tesson’: Drinks like a 1er Cru. There’s richness coupled with finesse. White peach fruit is tinged with spice. It has great shape in the mouth, with a fruit sweet heart and great drive and length.

2016 Domaine Roulot Meursault 1er Cru Les Poruzots: Rich and heady with notes of white peach and honeysuckle. There’s excellent presence in the mouth, a seriously chalky base and good power.

2016 Domaine Roulot Meursault 1er Cru Charmes: Complex aromatics of green melon, white peach, flint and citrus. It is rich and layered in the mouth. Acidity is relatively gentle, but it finishes with good energy and is super-long.

2016 Domaine Roulot Meursault 1er Cru Clos des Bouchères: Expressive and rich, with candied citrus fruits on the nose and in the mouth. It is sappy and concentrated, a wine of authority, drive and outstanding length.

2016 Domaine Roulot Meursault 1er Cru Les Perrières: Not affected by frost in ’16 and what a wine. It is so fine, detailed and compact. You feel every rocky bump in the wine and there’s citrus and white peach fruits of astounding purity. It is intense and crackles with energy. The finish drives on and on.

2012 Domaine Roulot Meursault 1er Cru Clos des Bouchères: There’s a gentle whiff of toast along with rockmelon and citrus. It has good volume in the mouth and is very fresh, oozing minerality on the finish.

2011 Domaine Roulot Meursault 1er Cru Clos des Bouchères: Has some smoky mineral reduction and a core of sweet orchard fruits. It is quite rich and creamy and underpinned by a line of bright, mineral acidity.

2007 Domaine Roulot Meursault Les Tillets: Smells of honeysuckle, white peach and almond butter. It is full, complex and creamy but with good detail and tension. It is fresh and extremely long, drinking beautifully.

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked: That '16 Roulot MP was one of my whites of the trip, superb wine!

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: Ohhh can’t wait to get my hands on some!! I heard David is no longer at Roulot. Who is managing the day to day now?

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Apologies for the tardiness on getting notes up from our April trip. Here’s Roumier.

For years we have driven past the Roumier digs, longing to step inside the magic garden and descend into the cave of Chambolle loveliness. This year, we finally got a call up to visit and taste and it was worth the wait. Christophe Roumier was our most amiable host and we thoroughly enjoyed his company and insights on the wines. He seemed genuinely impressed to have met Colin, the hero from many repast editions and we look forward to being able to visit again soon.

The ‘17’s from barrel are so pure and inviting. They remind Christophe a little of the ‘14’s but with more tannin. The ‘17’s were partially de-stemmed with the Grand Crus being around 60% whole bunch down to the village wines at around 35%.

2017 Domaine G. Roumier Chambolle-Musigny: Malo had just started but you get a feel for how good this village wine is. The purest of cherry fruit has real depth and crunch.

2017 Domaine G. Roumier Morey St. Denis 1er Cru Clos de La Bussière: The malo hadn’t started and sweet berry fruit was tinged with floral spice. There’s mineral at this wine’s core and some grip to the long finish.

2017 Domaine G. Roumier Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Cras: Malo had just started. A very pretty wine, bursting with red fruits and flora. It has good depth in the mouth with a rich vein of minerality.

2017 Christophe Roumier Ruchottes-Chambertin, Grand Cru: A little darker in its fruit profile. There’s flesh underpinned by tannin. It is quite tight and linear with a long, mineral flecked finish.

2017 Domaine G. Roumier Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Amoureuses: Malo is about half way through. This has exquisite balance and a gorgeous aroma and flavour profile of red berry, pomegranate, violets and mineral. It has stunning precision and is long and strong with a velvety feel.

2017 Domaine G. Roumier Bonnes Mares, Grand Cru: Powerful and intense. So tight and nervy. A ball of vinous kinetic potential. Complex and quite brilliant.

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked: How many more chapters of the April travelogue may we expect, mate?

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Plenty. Hope to have a bit of time over the next few weeks to add a few reports.

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked: Hud’em Oelong, look forward to reading them.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Roumier is the language of god, all else is poor translation. At least, that’s what Rumi WOULD have written if he’d tasted at Roumi

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Olivier Lamy noted that he has less wine from 2017 than 2016, and ‘16 was light-on. The ripeness was there in ’17, with sugars at a similar level as 2015, but he wines have more acidity. They were certainly a joy to try from barrel and Olivier can be justifiably proud of what he has in the cellar.

2017 Hubert Lamy Bourgogne Blanc Les Chataigners: Ripe and generous, crammed full of orchard fruits. Tangy acidity jolts through the wine. Excellent for its level.

2017 Hubert Lamy Saint-Aubin La Princée: Yields were 70% down here. It shows plenty of preserved citrus and is ripe and dense. There’s a refreshing salty/mineral thing on the finish and it is long.

2017 Hubert Lamy Chassagne-Montrachet Le Concis du Champs: Some mineral reduction to the aroma. It is sappy and has good mid-palate sweetness and plenty of flesh. The ripe orchard fruit flavours stain the palate and the finish possesses good cut.

2017 Hubert Lamy Puligny-Montrachet Les Tremblots: Plenty of mineral here along with classic white flower and white peach notes synonymous with Puligny. It is rich and balanced.

2017 Hubert Lamy Santenay 1er Cru Clos des Gravières: Compact and tight for now, with green melon fruit and a huge squeeze of lemon. The finish is chalky and it is a wine of good detail and brightness.

2017 Hubert Lamy Saint-Aubin 1er Cru Clos de Meix: There’s ripe peach and citrus fruit here. It has good detail and presence. The wine tightens considerably on the back-end, displaying its linear build.

2017 Hubert Lamy Saint-Aubin 1er Cru Derrière Chez Edouard: Some mineral reduction to the aroma. It is rich and deep, full of citrussy flavour. It is linear and long with some white peach. There’s plenty of phenolic grip to the finish.

2017 Hubert Lamy Saint-Aubin 1er Cru Les Frionnes: There’s more generosity here but it is still elegant. There are notes of aniseed, white peach, apple and flint. It possesses excellent persistence.

2017 Hubert Lamy Saint-Aubin 1er Cru Clos de la Chateniere: There’s so much potential here. It has such great intensity with unctuous orchard fruits shrink wrapped hard against a mineral spine. It is chewy, chalky, rich and long.

2017 Hubert Lamy Saint-Aubin 1er Cru Les Murgers des Dents de Chien: The fruit profile here is of the yellow persuasion. There’s some spice and good richness. It really builds through the palate to a crescendo of sappiness on the long finish.

2017 Hubert Lamy Saint-Aubin 1er Cru En Remilly: Full of preserved citrus and salty minerals. There’s a sweet entry, dry finish and loads of sappy, rich fruit in between. Super wine with outstanding length.

2017 Hubert Lamy Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Macherelles: Rich, round and plump with a fruit-sweet heart. The finish is fresh and vibrant.

2017 Hubert Lamy Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Chaumées: Intense nose of orchard fruit and chalk. It is dense, textural, layered and long. A wine of outstanding proportion and balance.

2017 Hubert Lamy Puligny-Montrachet Les Tremblots Cuvée Haute Densité: So fine and floral on the nose with the purest white peach playing in the background. It has laser-like focus in the mouth, with great shape and a long, sappy finish.

2017 Hubert Lamy Saint-Aubin 1er Cru Derrière Chez Edouard Cuvée Haute Densité: Super-dense and chewy with concentration of citrus extract. It is so deep and rocky, possessing awesome cut to the extremely long finish. It’s going to be something special and its going to need a lot of time to unfurl.

2017 Hubert Lamy Criots-Bâtard-Montrachet Cuvée Haute Densité, Grand Cru: Rich, layered and explosive. Loads of mineral, spice and orchard fruits. So much volume and so persistent. Brilliant!

We finished with a few older things from the cellar.

2011 Hubert Lamy Saint-Aubin 1er Cru Clos de la Chateniere: There’s some rockmelon to the aroma and flavour profile along with white peach, lemon and spice. It is mid-weight with a tangy acid spine. The finish has chalky grip.

2010 Hubert Lamy Saint-Aubin 1er Cru Les Frionnes: Rich, with lightly honeyed stone fruits. It is intense, voluminous and long.

2005 Hubert Lamy Bourgogne Blanc Les Chataigners: Ridiculously good for its level. Ripe peach fruits have a little toasty development. It is rich, round and intense with plenty of flavour and good drive.

posts.name: Carter Y.
posts.cooked: Thank you for the TNs, Jeremy. I missed the chance on the Edouard HD thihs year, won’t let that happen again next year…

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: Lamy tell you, that was a great report. Hubert the top!

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Noel Ramonet hurtled down the stairs towards us looking very much like an unmade bed. He has a kind of Keith Richards façade and you just know at times he has pushed his body to the limit. He has a crackling energy, much like his wines and loves Rugby. As soon as we let on we were Aussies and was straight onto the topic of the Wallabies. He passed us onto his assistant with ‘Campese like’ quick hands and we were treated to a wonderful visit.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Bouzeron-Aligoté: Some mineral reduction and intense lemon fruit. Great line and poise.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Bourgogne Blanc: Peach fruits have a touch of aniseed spice. It has good shape and is cool and long. Excellent wine for its level.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet: This is an expansive wine with a high degree of elegance. There is some of the Ramonet spearmint showing on the nose. It has pure white peach fruit and excellent balance, with a fine and fresh finish.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Puligny-Montrachet: Ripe, with a lightly candied feel to the flavour profile. Rich and round. Sappy on the back-end.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Saint-Aubin 1er Cru Le Charmois: There’s richness here with tension. It has notes of smoky mineral and pure white peach. The palate is laden with salty mineral things and length is good.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru La Boudriotte: Engaging nose of orange blossom, flint and aniseed. It is relatively compact and tight, with good minerally detail.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Morgeot: More spice than the Boudriotte along with a richer mid-palate. It is dense and full but by no means heavy. Ripe, sappy peach flavour envelop the palate and the finish is cool with a stony feel.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Caillerets: Complex and finer than the preceding wines. More mineral and plenty of citrus and flora. Great intensity with superb cut and a long, chalky finish.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Ruchottes: Usually of Grand Cru quality for mine and this ’16 is no different. It has fabulous richness coupled with precision. There’s a core of dense, sappy white peach and some spearmint and mineral. It has great drive and is oh so long.

2015 Jean-Claude Ramonet Bienvenues-Bâtard-Montrachet, Grand Cru: Has the classic Bienvenues honeysuckle thing going on. There’s a whiff of toast and pure white peach too. It is generous and rich, but still very fresh and energetic. Lovely poise.

2015 Jean-Claude Ramonet Bâtard-Montrachet, Grand Cru: Intense, powerful and generous, with ripe orchard fruits and plenty of spearmint. It is layered and creamy, with explosive fruit flavours. It is an extremely deep and long wine. There is so much material here.

2015 Jean-Claude Ramonet Montrachet, Grand Cru: Butter, peach, aniseed, mint and chalk. Great complexity and layer upon layer of fruit, mineral and flora in the mouth. It is extremely rich and powerful yet light on its feet. The texture is unctuous and length phenomenal. A great Ramonet Monty.

2016 Domaine Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet Rouge: Generously fruited, with sweet cherry aromas and flavours trimmed by earth.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Morgeot Rouge: Red currant and cherry fruits overlay a chalky base. There good intensity and plenty of structure.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Clos St. Jean Rouge: All crunchy red fruits with some floral spice. It has good flesh and is bright and juicy. It finishes with great energy.

2016 Jean-Claude Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Clos de la Boudriotte Rouge: Fruits vacillate between red and black. It is tight, minerally, compact and long. There’s plenty here upon which to age.

posts.name: Dave Nerland
posts.cooked: Jeremy, as usually great notes. Curious, ever had Bruno Collin Chevalier Montrachet? Opened a 2016 Bruno Collin Chevalier Montrachet blind for the group I taste with. So clean and mesmerizing…

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Never seen a Chevalier from Bruno Colin Dave. I have had a few bottles of their '11 Chaumées and '11 Maltroie of late, both drinking wonderfully well.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
kristofstevens:

From the 2017’s you’ve tasted, how would you describe the vintage in red and white? For red most vignerons compared to 2007 but I must admit I didn’t tast 2007 at this early stage so this is difficult for me to judge. I can say that the sappy fruit will make probably wines that will drink much easier than iether 15 or 16. For white we tasted much less, the wines were ripe like 15 but with more balance and acidity.

Not Jeremy, but, for the reds: 2000.

Harder to say for the whites.

As I recall, the 2000s to me seemed very light when first released, but put on weight about a year later. I thought the 2017s had more weight to them than the 2000s did at the same stage - more like the 2000s about a year after release [obviously, some difference by producer]. Makes me hopeful that the wines will have a similar style to 2000s but be better than the earlier vintage.

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
Never seen a Chevalier from Bruno Colin Dave. I have had a few bottles of their '11 Chaumées and '11 Maltroie of late, both drinking wonderfully well.

Can’t speak on Bruno’s Chaumee, but can concur on thought for Philippe’s version of 11 Chaumees. From 2 weeks ago in Atlanta:

2011 Philippe Colin Chassagne Montrachet Les Chaumees

This was wound pretty tight when opened but after a couple hours’ air and some time in the glass, started to unwind with tart apple, a little baked bread and some minerality. There is marked precision here, without being overtly acidic. Good, polished, focused wine.


At the same party, the 14 Ramonet St. Aubin En Remilly made an appearance in mag format:

2014 Jean-Claude Ramonet St. Aubin En Remilly

From mag, plenty of cool, steely notes in the aroma. Palate is nicely flavourful and has some drive, especially on a second taste when lemon custard and key lime pie take their place.


Keep the notes coming, Jeremy!

Mike

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Mike,

I really like where a lot of '11’s are at the moment and '14’s are just so good. Thank you for your notes.

Cheers
Jeremy
